name: Cova des Bufador de Son Berenguer
starting_point: From Santa Maria del Camí, take the Coanegra path to the Alaró path junction, take the first left to an electric transformer. Park near a beige building about 60m away.
equipment: Only a small rope may be needed at one visible point; may be necessary in winter.
description: A structural cave formed over Jurassic breccias (Lias) featuring large-scale collapses, a magnificent fault mirror, and over 1,000m of passages across twelve large spaces. Discovered in 1950, the cave spans 70m of depth with abundant crystalline formations and varied speleothems in its deepest chambers. Access requires permission from the landowner.
full_description:
Geologically speaking, the Bufador de Son Berenguer is situated over Jurassic breccias (Lias), being a structural cavity in which large-scale collapses have occurred. It is easy to observe at different points in the cavity large, threatening blocks ready to fall at any moment as a result of the aforementioned collapses. Some of them occurred so long ago that the fallen blocks are now completely calcified. It is also worth highlighting a magnificent fault mirror related to the mentioned collapses.

Discovered and explored on April 6, 1950, according to J. Mascaró (CTM., 1968), a team of speleologists from the GES studied it in the summer of 1960. The results were published in Speleon, 13 (Oviedo), in 1962, by J. Montoriol, who describes the clastic processes, the lithochemical intensity, the sedimentological aspects, and the solifluction derived from the floor settlements. He proposes the speleogenesis as an interstratigraphic development induced by waters absorbed from the exterior and offers several cross-sectional profiles of the cavity.

In 2002, J. A. Encinas, M. L. Redondo, and M. Luque surveyed the only topography currently existing of the cavity. In it, a notable ramp covered with endogenous clastic detritus can be seen, precipitated along the seventy meters of elevation from the entrance to the bottom, with large boulders obstructing the flanks and the bottom. They are Triassic limestone rocks, intensely leptoclased in the upper part, alternating with breccia strata and compact marls, as far as is perceptible inside. A cupular phenomenon of subsidence origin, of which we only see barely half of its more than 120 meters of virtual diameter, is arrested by a rectilinear break and by the travertine massif that runs from NW to NE. So that the vault remains largely stable thanks to the proliferation of columns derived from the intense lithochemical apparatus.

The interior route amply exceeds one thousand meters. It forms twelve large spaces between the two detached interstratigraphic plates that overlap in the first upper third. Part of these recesses are formed by the wedging of boulders retained between ceiling and floor, upon detaching voluminous calcareous sections. So that a good part of the journey is rough, though entertaining.

The most remote areas of the cavern possess abundant crystalline filigrees and notable speleothematic variety; as they require being traversed largely by crawling, the risk of breaking the formations became overwhelming during the obligatory planimetric work. More comfortable are the two upper chambers, also with a relatively low ceiling, with a fairly flat floor in the first and the other narrowing toward the east; while the open spaces at the bottom are the largest.

The vestibular or entrance zone, together with the southern extremity, serves as a preamble to the center of the complex. From there, four more or less passable paths depart, practicable without the need for ropes. The first toward the -61 m level, with an alternative path running through the columns that lead directly to the chaotic core of blocks, passing through a narrow crack. A third progression route toward the bottom heads NE above the flowstones, and the fourth requires climbing to the upper part of the right flank, as one enters the underground.

Regarding the formation of the endokarstic system, the determining argument was already documented in the proceedings of the "II National Congress of Speleology" held in 1972: J. A. Encinas, "A speleogenetic constant in the Pollensa area (Mallorca)." The note warned about the presence of plastic marl strata relatable to incipient cavernments, in discrete but illustrative and easily graspable calcareous cavities.

Unlike the general breccia behavior of the Triassic calcareous and dolomitic strata, and that of diaclastic discontinuities in the Burdigalian detrital materials, the vacuities generated at the contact between marly clays and compact rocks follow singular pneumatological patterns. Active in the initial chamber, when subjected to an atmosphere with high CO2 content. But, for this primordial space to be an effective excavatory instrument, the essential requirement is that it has sufficient volumetric entity to harbor dissolving capacity of the upper stratum.

Among the multiple possibilities for creating underground discontinuities, the one generated as a consequence of water mass loss in the marls — by basin adjustment, by water table migration, by vadose supply desiccation, or by sea level drop — entails notable reduction of clay volume. These shrink toward the bottom, causing the detachment of the drier upper parts and progressive subsidence of the plastic material, until reaching the hard rock plane: leaving empty the space corresponding to the volume of water expelled from the clays, or causing detachment of the rocks above, or both phenomena simultaneously. Dissolution thus begins the digestive process of the interior, and as it advances, it captures rainwater runoff that, in turn, moistens part of the already dried clays, drags them toward the drainage outlet, or sediments them on the bed, around the incipient detrital cone. Except when the subsidence undercutting causes the total detachment of the affected area, crushing the marl substrate and leaving clear a subcircular hemispherical contour; of which the speleologist usually only accesses the part above the detrital cone.

And the core of the emptying — whether clastic, massive, or ultimately digested by endokarstic dissolution — does not always manifest in its characteristic spheroid appearance, because frequently it is found in fan shape, or adopting a flattened labyrinthine arrangement, or, as in the case that specifically concerns us, constituting a steep ramp.

It is true that not all these ramps are due to focal subsidence processes, nor are all those effectively related to marls foreign to pre-Tortonian orogenic tensions; because the cave of Es Bufador, like the Cova Nova de Son Lluís (M.:Por./N.01), like the Coves de Gènova (M.:Pal./G.02) or the caves of Campanet (M.:Cpe./C.01), to cite only some highly significant phenomena, shows — as J. Montoriol already pointed out — an undeniable structural component. The same one in which a calcareous stratigraphic series slides over the more modern plastic marl deposits, causing an imbricate drag in which the percolating water action eventually takes its toll, along the axial plane of the contact; until draining beneath the ravine bed.

Note: Currently, the cave can only be accessed by requesting permission from the owner.
